addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  Hazard-closure — closes the recorded near-miss class where a PASSING fast suite spends money and mutates the depot.
  Small surface: one gate at the token-mint chokepoint, one suite-runner export, one proof case, prose.
  Tier: sonnet-delegable with care.
  
  ## Goal
  Make fast-tier credlessness structural.
  The fast suite runner sets the credless-guard tweak on every case invocation,
  and the OAuth/JWT token-mint chokepoint (the rbgo/rba path) dies instantly under it
  with its own band rejection code naming the violation.
  This establishes the suite invariant from the BUS0 tweak doctrine's slot-reservation rule:
  fast cases carry no tweaks of their own — in the fast tier the slot belongs to the guard.
  Add the doctrine-pure proof case:
  a deliberate cloud-verb invocation under the guard asserting the guard's specific band code.
  State the convention in the theurge/test authoring context where an author of a new fast case will read it.
  
  ## Open at mount
  Guard scope when fast fixtures run inside larger suites:
  the service/complete suites include the fast fixtures alongside credentialed and poison-tweak fixtures,
  so decide whether the guard rides only fast-suite runs
  or every invocation of a fast-tier fixture's cases regardless of hosting suite —
  the invariant (fast cases carry no tweaks of their own) holds either way.
  
  ## Cinched
  - The guard rides the single tweak slot; no new mechanism.
    The demolition condition is the doctrine's own: a genuine dual-tweak need mints the dedicated variable then.
  - The gate lands at token mint, gating actual credential use — never at zipper/dispatch; zippers untouched.
  - The guard's rejection code is a band code from the bubc tinder block.
  
  ## Sources
  Memos/memo-20260610-heat-BH-fast-tier-credless-by-convention.md
  (the near-miss record; the spot-fix seam it describes retires earlier in this heat).
  
  ## Done when
  A fast case reaching token mint dies with the guard's band code by construction;
  the proof case asserts that code;
  fast suite green;
  the convention documented in the test authoring context.

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣Be
2	
3	## Gate
4	
5	Cleared (2026-06-12): the bash-filename case-consolidation landed and its heat retired;
6	this heat's docket file references have been refreshed to the lowercase successors.
7	This heat runs comfortably in a parallel repo alongside ₣Bb.
8	
9	## Shape
10	
11	Two strands, one gestalt: doctrine-pure negative testing.
12	
13	Strand one — named refusals.
14	Theurge negative cases assert bare nonzero exit,
15	so a case expecting deliberate rejection also passes on any harness breakage
16	(unbound variable, missing file, refactor typo) —
17	the fast-tier fixtures carry this wrong-reason hole across their negative cases.
18	The fix: a small precision exit-code band, allocated per rejection gate
19	(roughly half a dozen codes; the census at the 2026-06-11 groom counted 6–8 gates),
20	homed as tinder in bubc,
21	projected into the theurge Rust consts by the existing zipper codegen,
22	and asserted specifically by the negative helpers.
23	
24	Strand two — the tweak channel re-grounded (added at the 2026-06-11 groom).
25	The BUS0 Tweak Mechanism doctrine (landed at heat start) states what a tweak is for:
26	force one hard-to-produce condition for a test to observe handled correctly;
27	one tweak at a time per test/fixture/suite, by design;
28	a suite may reserve the slot for a standing guard.
29	This heat brings the live census into conformance:
30	the two non-conforming tweaks retire
31	(the immure convenience short-circuit becomes a real read-only dry-run colophon;
32	the graft parameter injection becomes the hallmark-installer election chain,
33	riding the existing hallmark fact + previous-dir chaining machinery),
34	the fast tier reserves the slot for the credless guard
35	(closing the recorded near-miss class: a passing fast suite that spends money and mutates the depot,
36	gated at the token-mint chokepoint with a band code),
37	and the regime-validation negatives convert from fabricated files driven through
38	test-only `*_probate` side doors to in-universe poisoning —
39	real validate verbs against real regimes, one BUK regime-load seam with set/unset semantics —
40	housed in a new fixture that runs in every suite above fast
41	(fast holds the guard; poison cases need the slot; the two never share a run).
42	
43	Work order: census cleanup first (band-independent),
44	then band membrane + numeric codegen,
45	then the guard (its rejection code needs the band),
46	then the conversions, prose last.
47	
48	## Cinched
49	
50	- Exit-code band over stderr sentinel:
51	  a sentinel is string interpretation minimized, not avoided,
52	  and is swallowed by the same wrappers that launder codes.
53	- Per-gate codes, never per-rule —
54	  the hole being closed is wrong-layer failure, not wrong-rule failure.
55	- Allocation rule: gates may share a code only if they never co-occur
56	  in one test case's spawn path — share across alternatives, never along a pipeline.
57	- `buc_die` propagates in-band `$?` values instead of remapping to 1,
58	  so existing `|| buc_die` call sites need no audit or change.
59	- No code minted outside the bubc tinder block.
60	- One tweak at a time is doctrine, not limitation;
61	  a dedicated constraint variable mints only when a genuine dual-tweak need survives scrutiny —
62	  never by widening the single slot.
63	- Fast carries the credless guard in the tweak slot;
64	  fast cases carry no tweaks of their own.
65	  A case that needs a seam has self-identified as not belonging in fast.
66	- In-universe over fabrication: negatives run real verbs against real regimes wherever the
67	  injection point is a value; the probate side doors delete with no parallel survival.
68	- The guard gates token mint (actual credential use), never zipper/dispatch — zippers untouched.
69	- The hallmark installer is graft-slot election only;
70	  any general election verb belongs to the made-side retrofit heat.
71	
72	## Out of scope
73	
74	- tadmor-security's containment cases:
75	  exit codes there cross docker-exec boundaries with their own laundering rules —
76	  a different problem, a different heat if it ever itches.
77	- enrollment-validation needs no in-universe conversion:
78	  it already stages values inline against the validator with no file fabrication and no tweak;
79	  it takes only the band-code assertion migration.
80	- recipe-validation and dockerfile-hygiene fabrication is honest:
81	  the artifact under test IS a file; they take only the band-code migration.
82	
83	## Done when
84	
85	Every fast-tier negative case asserts a specific band code,
86	the survival proof lives in the BUK self-test,
87	the tweak census conforms to the BUS0 doctrine (stamp, poison seam, guard — nothing else),
88	no regime `*_probate` side door survives,
89	fast is credless by construction,
90	and the BCG admission entry documents band semantics and the allocation rule.

92	# jjezs_pace ₢BeAAI

95	Hazard-closure — closes the recorded near-miss class where a PASSING fast suite spends money and mutates the depot.
96	Small surface: one gate at the token-mint chokepoint, one suite-runner export, one proof case, prose.
97	Tier: sonnet-delegable with care.
98	
99	## Goal
100	Make fast-tier credlessness structural.
101	The fast suite runner sets the credless-guard tweak on every case invocation,
102	and the OAuth/JWT token-mint chokepoint (the rbgo/rba path) dies instantly under it
103	with its own band rejection code naming the violation.
104	This establishes the suite invariant from the BUS0 tweak doctrine's slot-reservation rule:
105	fast cases carry no tweaks of their own — in the fast tier the slot belongs to the guard.
106	Add the doctrine-pure proof case:
107	a deliberate cloud-verb invocation under the guard asserting the guard's specific band code.
108	State the convention in the theurge/test authoring context where an author of a new fast case will read it.
109	
110	## Open at mount
111	Guard scope when fast fixtures run inside larger suites:
112	the service/complete suites include the fast fixtures alongside credentialed and poison-tweak fixtures,
113	so decide whether the guard rides only fast-suite runs
114	or every invocation of a fast-tier fixture's cases regardless of hosting suite —
115	the invariant (fast cases carry no tweaks of their own) holds either way.
116	
117	## Cinched

119	  The demolition condition is the doctrine's own: a genuine dual-tweak need mints the dedicated variable then.
120	- The gate lands at token mint, gating actual credential use — never at zipper/dispatch; zippers untouched.
121	- The guard's rejection code is a band code from the bubc tinder block.

# jjezs_paddock ₣Be

## Gate

Cleared (2026-06-12): the bash-filename case-consolidation landed and its heat retired;
this heat's docket file references have been refreshed to the lowercase successors.
This heat runs comfortably in a parallel repo alongside ₣Bb.

## Shape

Two strands, one gestalt: doctrine-pure negative testing.

Strand one — named refusals.
Theurge negative cases assert bare nonzero exit,
so a case expecting deliberate rejection also passes on any harness breakage
(unbound variable, missing file, refactor typo) —
the fast-tier fixtures carry this wrong-reason hole across their negative cases.
The fix: a small precision exit-code band, allocated per rejection gate
(roughly half a dozen codes; the census at the 2026-06-11 groom counted 6–8 gates),
homed as tinder in bubc,
projected into the theurge Rust consts by the existing zipper codegen,
and asserted specifically by the negative helpers.

Strand two — the tweak channel re-grounded (added at the 2026-06-11 groom).
The BUS0 Tweak Mechanism doctrine (landed at heat start) states what a tweak is for:
force one hard-to-produce condition for a test to observe handled correctly;
one tweak at a time per test/fixture/suite, by design;
a suite may reserve the slot for a standing guard.
This heat brings the live census into conformance:
the two non-conforming tweaks retire
(the immure convenience short-circuit becomes a real read-only dry-run colophon;
the graft parameter injection becomes the hallmark-installer election chain,
riding the existing hallmark fact + previous-dir chaining machinery),
the fast tier reserves the slot for the credless guard
(closing the recorded near-miss class: a passing fast suite that spends money and mutates the depot,
gated at the token-mint chokepoint with a band code),
and the regime-validation negatives convert from fabricated files driven through
test-only `*_probate` side doors to in-universe poisoning —
real validate verbs against real regimes, one BUK regime-load seam with set/unset semantics —
housed in a new fixture that runs in every suite above fast
(fast holds the guard; poison cases need the slot; the two never share a run).

Work order: census cleanup first (band-independent),
then band membrane + numeric codegen,
then the guard (its rejection code needs the band),
then the conversions, prose last.

## Cinched

- Exit-code band over stderr sentinel:
  a sentinel is string interpretation minimized, not avoided,
  and is swallowed by the same wrappers that launder codes.
- Per-gate codes, never per-rule —
  the hole being closed is wrong-layer failure, not wrong-rule failure.
- Allocation rule: gates may share a code only if they never co-occur
  in one test case's spawn path — share across alternatives, never along a pipeline.
- `buc_die` propagates in-band `$?` values instead of remapping to 1,
  so existing `|| buc_die` call sites need no audit or change.
- No code minted outside the bubc tinder block.
- One tweak at a time is doctrine, not limitation;
  a dedicated constraint variable mints only when a genuine dual-tweak need survives scrutiny —
  never by widening the single slot.
- Fast carries the credless guard in the tweak slot;
  fast cases carry no tweaks of their own.
  A case that needs a seam has self-identified as not belonging in fast.
- In-universe over fabrication: negatives run real verbs against real regimes wherever the
  injection point is a value; the probate side doors delete with no parallel survival.
- The guard gates token mint (actual credential use), never zipper/dispatch — zippers untouched.
- The hallmark installer is graft-slot election only;
  any general election verb belongs to the made-side retrofit heat.

## Out of scope

- tadmor-security's containment cases:
  exit codes there cross docker-exec boundaries with their own laundering rules —
  a different problem, a different heat if it ever itches.
- enrollment-validation needs no in-universe conversion:
  it already stages values inline against the validator with no file fabrication and no tweak;
  it takes only the band-code assertion migration.
- recipe-validation and dockerfile-hygiene fabrication is honest:
  the artifact under test IS a file; they take only the band-code migration.

## Done when

Every fast-tier negative case asserts a specific band code,
the survival proof lives in the BUK self-test,
the tweak census conforms to the BUS0 doctrine (stamp, poison seam, guard — nothing else),
no regime `*_probate` side door survives,
fast is credless by construction,
and the BCG admission entry documents band semantics and the allocation rule.

# jjezs_pace ₢BeAAI

## Character
Hazard-closure — closes the recorded near-miss class where a PASSING fast suite spends money and mutates the depot.
Small surface: one gate at the token-mint chokepoint, one suite-runner export, one proof case, prose.
Tier: sonnet-delegable with care.

## Goal
Make fast-tier credlessness structural.
The fast suite runner sets the credless-guard tweak on every case invocation,
and the OAuth/JWT token-mint chokepoint (the rbgo/rba path) dies instantly under it
with its own band rejection code naming the violation.
This establishes the suite invariant from the BUS0 tweak doctrine's slot-reservation rule:
fast cases carry no tweaks of their own — in the fast tier the slot belongs to the guard.
Add the doctrine-pure proof case:
a deliberate cloud-verb invocation under the guard asserting the guard's specific band code.
State the convention in the theurge/test authoring context where an author of a new fast case will read it.

## Open at mount
Guard scope when fast fixtures run inside larger suites:
the service/complete suites include the fast fixtures alongside credentialed and poison-tweak fixtures,
so decide whether the guard rides only fast-suite runs
or every invocation of a fast-tier fixture's cases regardless of hosting suite —
the invariant (fast cases carry no tweaks of their own) holds either way.

## Cinched
- The guard rides the single tweak slot; no new mechanism.
  The demolition condition is the doctrine's own: a genuine dual-tweak need mints the dedicated variable then.
- The gate lands at token mint, gating actual credential use — never at zipper/dispatch; zippers untouched.
- The guard's rejection code is a band code from the bubc tinder block.

## Sources
Memos/memo-20260610-heat-BH-fast-tier-credless-by-convention.md
(the near-miss record; the spot-fix seam it describes retires earlier in this heat).

## Done when
A fast case reaching token mint dies with the guard's band code by construction;
the proof case asserts that code;
fast suite green;
the convention documented in the test authoring context.

content.text:
Mounted ₢BeAAI (fast-credless-guard): make fast-tier credlessness structural — the fast suite runner sets the credless-guard tweak on every case, and the OAuth/JWT token-mint chokepoint dies under it with its own band rejection code, plus a proof case asserting that code and a line of convention prose.

Heat context: ₣Be (rbk-09-better-negatives), 4/10 done. The band membrane and numeric codegen landed in the last two paces, so the guard's rejection code (`BUBC_band_credless` → `RBTDGC_BAND_CREDLESS`, code 104 territory) already exists on both sides of the shell/Rust seam — this pace consumes it.

One open decision flagged for mount: whether the guard rides only fast-*suite* runs, or every invocation of a fast-tier fixture's cases even when hosted in service/complete suites. My lean is the latter (per-fixture-tier, not per-suite) — it makes the invariant a property of the fixture rather than the run, so a fast fixture can never mint a token no matter who hosts it. Happy to expand the trade-off if you want it.

Silks fit the docket fine. Approach: sequential, this session — small surface (one gate in the rbgo/rba mint path, one runner export, one proof case, prose), but the seam placement wants care.

Proceed?

message.content: go!  per fixture is a good idea

content.text: Per-fixture it is — the guard is a property of the fixture's tier, not the hosting suite. Starting by reading the band constants, the memo, and the token-mint path.

22	
23	# Guard against multiple inclusion
24	test -z "${ZBUBC_SOURCED:-}" || return 0
25	ZBUBC_SOURCED=1
26	
27	# Source-time literal constants.
28	#
29	# BUBC_moorings_dir is the basename of the project's config dir. The
30	# project-intimate trampoline (z-launcher) is the sole authority for the name;
31	# it exports BURD_CONFIG_DIR (absolute), and we derive the basename here so
32	# tooling (qualify, tabtarget creation) refers to it without re-literaling.
33	BUBC_moorings_dir="${BURD_CONFIG_DIR##*/}"
34	BUBC_launchers_subdir="rbml_launchers"
35	BUBC_rbmn_nodes_subdir="rbmn_nodes"
36	BUBC_rbmu_users_subdir="rbmu_users"
37	
38	# Platform identifiers (bunne_* — BURN node-regime enum sprue family).
39	# These values are the canonical OS-family identifiers used in BURN_PLATFORM
40	# enrollment and in per-tabtarget platform invariant assertions.
41	# BUBC_platforms_<family> tinder constants provide single source of truth so
42	# code refers to the identifier by family name rather than hardcoding the
43	# literal token at every comparison site.
44	BUBC_platforms_linux="bunne_linux"
45	BUBC_platforms_mac="bunne_mac"
46	BUBC_platforms_windows="bunne_windows"
47	
48	# Windows OpenSSH layout — forward slashes throughout so identical strings
49	# work in PowerShell, terminal display, and icacls invocations.
50	BUBC_windows_sshd_config='C:/ProgramData/ssh/sshd_config'
51	BUBC_windows_admin_auth_keys='C:/ProgramData/ssh/administrators_authorized_keys'
52	BUBC_windows_ssh_port="22"
53	BUBC_windows_fw_rule_name="sshd"
54	BUBC_windows_fw_display_name="OpenSSH Server"
55	
56	# Precision exit-code band — deliberate-rejection gate codes.
57	# An in-band exit status means a named rejection gate fired on purpose;
58	# exit 1 stays "imprecise death" (buc_die default). buc_die propagates
59	# in-band $? values unchanged (the band membrane), so existing
60	# `cmd || buc_die` chains carry these codes to the dispatch boundary where
61	# the test orchestrator asserts them in negative cases.
62	# Placement: clear of shell-reserved codes (2, 126, 127, 128+n signals),
63	# the sysexits.h range (64-78), curl's exit codes (1-92), and
64	# timeout(1)/container-runtime reserved codes (124-125).
65	# Allocation rule: one code per rejection GATE, never per validation rule.
66	# Gates may share a code only if they never co-occur in one test case's
67	# spawn path — share across alternatives, never along a pipeline.
68	# No band code is minted outside this block.
69	BUBC_band_base=100
70	BUBC_band_width=16
71	# Gate codes, allocated upward from base:
72	BUBC_band_regime=100    # regime validation rejection (validate verbs)
73	BUBC_band_enroll=101    # enrollment validation rejection
74	BUBC_band_recipe=102    # recipe validation rejection
75	BUBC_band_hygiene=103   # Dockerfile FROM-line hygiene rejection (rbfh)
76	BUBC_band_credless=104  # credless guard at token mint (fast-tier suite invariant)
77	# Self-test probe pins the band top, proving full-width propagation:
78	BUBC_band_selftest=115  # BUK self-test deliberate rejection (buw-xb fixture)
79	
80	# Windows registry preconditions for unattended power-on posture.
81	# Operator-handbook step (BUSJHW Windows: Host Availability) sets these;
82	# bujb_invigilate_windows reads them. Single source of truth so the path
83	# the handbook tells the operator to set is the path invigilate queries.
84	# PowerShell-canonical form (HKLM:\ prefix, mixed case — registry is
85	# case-insensitive at the OS level so display case is purely cosmetic).
86	BUBC_windows_passwordless_path='HKLM:\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\PasswordLess\Device'
87	BUBC_windows_passwordless_value='DevicePasswordLessBuildVersion'
88	BUBC_windows_aoac_path='HKLM:\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power'
89	BUBC_windows_aoac_value='PlatformAoAcOverride'
90	
91	# eof
92

#
# BUBC — BUK Bootstrap Constants
# Source-time literal constants for BUK kit layout.
# No kindle dependency — available immediately upon sourcing.

# Guard against multiple inclusion
test -z "${ZBUBC_SOURCED:-}" || return 0
ZBUBC_SOURCED=1

# Source-time literal constants.
#
# BUBC_moorings_dir is the basename of the project's config dir. The
# project-intimate trampoline (z-launcher) is the sole authority for the name;
# it exports BURD_CONFIG_DIR (absolute), and we derive the basename here so
# tooling (qualify, tabtarget creation) refers to it without re-literaling.
BUBC_moorings_dir="${BURD_CONFIG_DIR##*/}"
BUBC_launchers_subdir="rbml_launchers"
BUBC_rbmn_nodes_subdir="rbmn_nodes"
BUBC_rbmu_users_subdir="rbmu_users"

# Platform identifiers (bunne_* — BURN node-regime enum sprue family).
# These values are the canonical OS-family identifiers used in BURN_PLATFORM
# enrollment and in per-tabtarget platform invariant assertions.
# BUBC_platforms_<family> tinder constants provide single source of truth so
# code refers to the identifier by family name rather than hardcoding the
# literal token at every comparison site.
BUBC_platforms_linux="bunne_linux"
BUBC_platforms_mac="bunne_mac"
BUBC_platforms_windows="bunne_windows"

# Windows OpenSSH layout — forward slashes throughout so identical strings
# work in PowerShell, terminal display, and icacls invocations.
BUBC_windows_sshd_config='C:/ProgramData/ssh/sshd_config'
BUBC_windows_admin_auth_keys='C:/ProgramData/ssh/administrators_authorized_keys'
BUBC_windows_ssh_port="22"
BUBC_windows_fw_rule_name="sshd"
BUBC_windows_fw_display_name="OpenSSH Server"

# Precision exit-code band — deliberate-rejection gate codes.
# An in-band exit status means a named rejection gate fired on purpose;
# exit 1 stays "imprecise death" (buc_die default). buc_die propagates
# in-band $? values unchanged (the band membrane), so existing
# `cmd || buc_die` chains carry these codes to the dispatch boundary where
# the test orchestrator asserts them in negative cases.
# Placement: clear of shell-reserved codes (2, 126, 127, 128+n signals),
# the sysexits.h range (64-78), curl's exit codes (1-92), and
# timeout(1)/container-runtime reserved codes (124-125).
# Allocation rule: one code per rejection GATE, never per validation rule.
# Gates may share a code only if they never co-occur in one test case's
# spawn path — share across alternatives, never along a pipeline.
# No band code is minted outside this block.
BUBC_band_base=100
BUBC_band_width=16
# Gate codes, allocated upward from base:
BUBC_band_regime=100    # regime validation rejection (validate verbs)
BUBC_band_enroll=101    # enrollment validation rejection
BUBC_band_recipe=102    # recipe validation rejection
BUBC_band_hygiene=103   # Dockerfile FROM-line hygiene rejection (rbfh)
BUBC_band_credless=104  # credless guard at token mint (fast-tier suite invariant)
# Self-test probe pins the band top, proving full-width propagation:
BUBC_band_selftest=115  # BUK self-test deliberate rejection (buw-xb fixture)

# Windows registry preconditions for unattended power-on posture.
# Operator-handbook step (BUSJHW Windows: Host Availability) sets these;
# bujb_invigilate_windows reads them. Single source of truth so the path
# the handbook tells the operator to set is the path invigilate queries.
# PowerShell-canonical form (HKLM:\ prefix, mixed case — registry is
# case-insensitive at the OS level so display case is purely cosmetic).
BUBC_windows_passwordless_path='HKLM:\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\PasswordLess\Device'
BUBC_windows_passwordless_value='DevicePasswordLessBuildVersion'
BUBC_windows_aoac_path='HKLM:\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Power'
BUBC_windows_aoac_value='PlatformAoAcOverride'

# eof

1	#!/bin/bash
2	#
3	# Copyright 2025 Scale Invariant, Inc.
4	#
5	# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
6	# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
7	# You may obtain a copy of the License at
8	#
9	#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
10	#
11	# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
12	# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
13	# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
14	# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
15	# limitations under the License.
16	#
17	# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
18	#
19	# BURE Regime - Bash Utility Regime Environment Module
20	#
21	# BURE is an ambient regime — variables are set in the environment by callers,
22	# not sourced from a file. Callers export BURE_* variables before invoking.
23	
24	set -euo pipefail
25	
26	# Multiple inclusion detection
27	test -z "${ZBURE_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module bure multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
28	ZBURE_SOURCED=1
29	
30	######################################################################
31	# Internal Functions (zbure_*)
32	
33	zbure_kindle() {
34	  test -z "${ZBURE_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module bure already kindled"
35	
36	  # No defaults set — buv uses ${!varname:-} for safe indirect expansion under set -u.
37	  # Unset variables are detected distinctly from empty by zbuv_check_capture.
38	  # Exception: ambient regime defaults for optional behavioral overrides.
39	  BURE_VERBOSE="${BURE_VERBOSE:-0}"
40	  BURE_COLOR="${BURE_COLOR:-auto}"
41	  BURE_COUNTDOWN="${BURE_COUNTDOWN:-}"
42	  BURE_TWEAK_NAME="${BURE_TWEAK_NAME:-}"

file.content:
#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2025 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# BURE Regime - Bash Utility Regime Environment Module
#
# BURE is an ambient regime — variables are set in the environment by callers,
# not sourced from a file. Callers export BURE_* variables before invoking.

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion detection
test -z "${ZBURE_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module bure multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
ZBURE_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (zbure_*)

zbure_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZBURE_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module bure already kindled"

  # No defaults set — buv uses ${!varname:-} for safe indirect expansion under set -u.
  # Unset variables are detected distinctly from empty by zbuv_check_capture.
  # Exception: ambient regime defaults for optional behavioral overrides.
  BURE_VERBOSE="${BURE_VERBOSE:-0}"
  BURE_COLOR="${BURE_COLOR:-auto}"
  BURE_COUNTDOWN="${BURE_COUNTDOWN:-}"
  BURE_TWEAK_NAME="${BURE_TWEAK_NAME:-}"
  BURE_TWEAK_VALUE="${BURE_TWEAK_VALUE:-}"
  BURE_LABEL="${BURE_LABEL:-}"

  # Enroll all BURE variables — single source of truth for validation and rendering

  buv_regime_enroll BURE

  buv_group_enroll "Behavioral Overrides"
  buv_string_enroll  BURE_COUNTDOWN   0  4  "Countdown override (skip to disable)"
  buv_enum_enroll    BURE_VERBOSE     "Verbosity level" 0 1 2 3
  buv_enum_enroll    BURE_COLOR       "Color mode" auto 0 1

  buv_group_enroll "Tweak Mechanism"
  buv_string_enroll  BURE_TWEAK_NAME  0  64   "Tweak name (buo-sprued; consumer-interpreted)"
  buv_string_enroll  BURE_TWEAK_VALUE 0  256  "Tweak value (consumer-interpreted)"

  buv_group_enroll "Exchange Labels"
  buv_string_enroll  BURE_LABEL       0  120  "Correlation label for cross-project exchange (xname format)"

  # Guard against unexpected BURE_ variables not in enrollment
  buv_scope_sentinel BURE BURE_

  # Lock all enrolled BURE_ variables against mutation
  buv_lock BURE

  readonly ZBURE_KINDLED=1
}

zbure_sentinel() {
  test "${ZBURE_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module bure not kindled - call zbure_kindle first"
}

# Enforce all BURE enrollment validations
zbure_enforce() {
  zbure_sentinel

  buv_vet BURE

  # Custom enforce: BURE_COUNTDOWN must be empty or "skip"
  if test -n "${BURE_COUNTDOWN}"; then
    test "${BURE_COUNTDOWN}" = "skip" \
      || buc_die "BURE_COUNTDOWN must be 'skip' or empty, got '${BURE_COUNTDOWN}'"
  fi

  # Custom enforce: BURE_TWEAK_NAME, when set, must carry the buo tweak sprue —
  # a `buo<segment>_` prefix. BUK validates the SHAPE only (the virtual registry
  # of tweaks is `grep buo`); it never enumerates consumer names, so this stays
  # generic. An unregistered or mistyped tweak name fails loud here rather than
  # silently no-op'ing at the consumer.
  # Doctrine (BUS0 "Tweak Mechanism"): a tweak forces one hard-to-produce
  # condition for a test to observe handled correctly; one tweak at a time per
  # test/fixture/suite, by design; a suite may reserve the slot for a standing guard.
  if test -n "${BURE_TWEAK_NAME}"; then
    case "${BURE_TWEAK_NAME}" in
      buo[a-z]*_*) : ;;
      *) buc_die "BURE_TWEAK_NAME must carry the buo sprue (buo<segment>_<name>), got '${BURE_TWEAK_NAME}'" ;;
    esac
  fi
}

# eof

1	# Fast-tier credlessness is convention, not construction — near-miss record
2	
3	Date: 2026-06-10
4	Status: standing-hazard record from the podvm refresh pace; the immediate instance was caught
5	pre-run and fixed with a buo test-seam. Triage material for the ₣BH terminal memo-walk pace.
6	TRIAGED 2026-06-10: slated as the fast-tier-credless-by-construction pace (₢BHAAl, pending — the poison-marker enforcement).
7	RESTAMPED 2026-06-11: ₢BHAAl dropped at the ₣Be groom; closure recreated as ₢BeAAI (fast-credless-guard) —
8	the guard rides the tweak slot per the BUS0 tweak doctrine's suite-reservation rule and dies at the
9	token-mint chokepoint with a band rejection code (₣Be precision exit-code band).
10	
11	## The near-miss
12	
13	The fast-tier podvm resolve case invokes the real `rbw-lI` tabtarget against the real working
14	regime — theurge isolates only BURV temp/output dirs, never credentials. On a credentialed
15	workstation (the operator's always is), the case would have sailed past its asserted diagnostic
16	into a live multi-GB Cloud Build submission, inside the `fast` suite, on every run.
17	Caught by inspection before any run; fixed by short-circuiting the verb under a
18	`buorb_immure_resolve_only` tweak.
19	
20	## The standing hazard
21	
22	Nothing structural makes the fast tier credless. Every existing fast fixture is safe only
23	because none happened to invoke a GCP-capable colophon — the first one that did nearly fired a
24	live build. Future fast cases can repeat this silently: the failure mode is a *passing* fast
25	suite that spends money and mutates the depot.
26	
27	## Triage question
28	
29	Whether to enforce fast-tier credlessness by construction. Candidate shapes (not designed, just
30	recorded): the fast suite runner exports a poison marker that the credential-load path refuses
31	(loud "fast tier reached a credential load" die); or theurge's fast tier asserts no network
32	egress; or a per-case declaration ("reaches GCP: yes/no") validated against the tier. Each has
33	costs; the buo-seam-per-case convention may also be judged sufficient — but then the convention
34	needs stating somewhere an author of a new fast case will read it.
35

file.content:
# Fast-tier credlessness is convention, not construction — near-miss record

Date: 2026-06-10
Status: standing-hazard record from the podvm refresh pace; the immediate instance was caught
pre-run and fixed with a buo test-seam. Triage material for the ₣BH terminal memo-walk pace.
TRIAGED 2026-06-10: slated as the fast-tier-credless-by-construction pace (₢BHAAl, pending — the poison-marker enforcement).
RESTAMPED 2026-06-11: ₢BHAAl dropped at the ₣Be groom; closure recreated as ₢BeAAI (fast-credless-guard) —
the guard rides the tweak slot per the BUS0 tweak doctrine's suite-reservation rule and dies at the
token-mint chokepoint with a band rejection code (₣Be precision exit-code band).

## The near-miss

The fast-tier podvm resolve case invokes the real `rbw-lI` tabtarget against the real working
regime — theurge isolates only BURV temp/output dirs, never credentials. On a credentialed
workstation (the operator's always is), the case would have sailed past its asserted diagnostic
into a live multi-GB Cloud Build submission, inside the `fast` suite, on every run.
Caught by inspection before any run; fixed by short-circuiting the verb under a
`buorb_immure_resolve_only` tweak.

## The standing hazard

Nothing structural makes the fast tier credless. Every existing fast fixture is safe only
because none happened to invoke a GCP-capable colophon — the first one that did nearly fired a
live build. Future fast cases can repeat this silently: the failure mode is a *passing* fast
suite that spends money and mutates the depot.

## Triage question

Whether to enforce fast-tier credlessness by construction. Candidate shapes (not designed, just
recorded): the fast suite runner exports a poison marker that the credential-load path refuses
(loud "fast tier reached a credential load" die); or theurge's fast tier asserts no network
egress; or a per-case declaration ("reaches GCP: yes/no") validated against the tier. Each has
costs; the buo-seam-per-case convention may also be judged sufficient — but then the convention
needs stating somewhere an author of a new fast case will read it.

16	//
17	// RBTDRI — tabtarget invocation layer for theurge
18	//
19	// Theurge invokes bottle operations exclusively through tabtargets, never
20	// reimplementing bash command logic. This module provides:
21	//
22	//   1. Tabtarget discovery — imprint-scoped, global, or nameplate-scoped
23	//   2. Tabtarget execution with BURV isolation — per-invocation output/temp dirs
24	//   3. Ifrit verdict parsing — extract verdict from ifrit stdout + exit code
25	//   4. BURV fact file reading — extract structured output from tabtarget results
26	
27	use std::path::{Path, PathBuf};
28	use std::process::Command;
29	use std::sync::OnceLock;
30	
31	use crate::rbtdre_engine::rbtdre_Verdict;
32	use crate::rbtdgc_consts::RBTDGC_CRUCIBLE_BARK;
33	use crate::rbtdrx_platform::{rbtdrx_is_cygwin, rbtdrx_native_to_posix, rbtdrx_posix_to_native};
34	
35	/// Ifrit binary name inside the bottle container.
36	const RBTDRI_IFRIT_BINARY: &str = "rbid";
37	
38	/// BUK dispatch output subdirectory — tabtargets write facts to BURV_OUTPUT_ROOT_DIR/current.
39	/// Matches BURD_OUTPUT_DIR = "${BURC_OUTPUT_ROOT_DIR}/current" from bud_dispatch.sh.
40	pub const RBTDRI_BURV_OUTPUT_SUBDIR: &str = "current";
41	
42	/// Env var name read by `buc_require` (buc_command.sh:335) to bypass interactive
43	/// confirmation prompts in non-interactive contexts (test fixtures, automation).
44	pub const RBTDRI_BURE_CONFIRM_KEY: &str = "BURE_CONFIRM";
45	
46	/// Value paired with `RBTDRI_BURE_CONFIRM_KEY` to skip the confirmation prompt.
47	pub const RBTDRI_BURE_CONFIRM_SKIP: &str = "skip";
48	
49	/// BUK dispatch env var carrying the temp root for theurge — anchors BURV
50	/// per-invoke temp dirs under temp-buk/ rather than /tmp/. Required: theurge
51	/// fails at startup if unset. Set by bud_dispatch.sh on every tabtarget call.
52	pub const RBTDRI_BURD_TEMP_DIR_KEY: &str = "BURD_TEMP_DIR";
53	
54	/// Canonical Cygwin bash in POSIX form. theurge nativizes this via RBTDRX
55	/// (cygpath) to launch scripts: a bare "bash" from a Windows-native binary
56	/// resolves through `CreateProcess` to System32's WSL launcher, never Cygwin's.
57	const RBTDRI_CYGWIN_BASH_POSIX: &str = "/bin/bash";
58	
59	/// BURV invoke-directory name from a zero-based invoke count.
60	///
61	/// Single source of truth for the `invoke-NNNNN` naming pattern; tests call
62	/// this rather than hand-expanding the literal.
63	pub fn rbtdri_invoke_dir_name(invoke_num: u32) -> String {
64	    format!("invoke-{:05}", invoke_num)
65	}
66	
67	// ── Invocation result ────────────────────────────────────────
68	
69	/// Captured output from a tabtarget invocation.
70	#[derive(Debug)]
71	pub struct rbtdri_InvokeResult {
72	    pub stdout: String,
73	    pub stderr: String,
74	    pub exit_code: i32,
75	    pub burv_output: PathBuf,
76	}
77	
78	// ── Invocation context ───────────────────────────────────────
79	
80	/// Per-case invocation context. Tracks BURV isolation state so each tabtarget
81	/// invocation within a case gets its own output and temp directories, matching
82	/// the zbuto_invoke() pattern from buto_operations.sh.
83	///
84	/// `fixture` carries the fixture name. For crucible fixtures (tadmor/srjcl/
85	/// pluml) the fixture name happens to equal a valid nameplate moniker — that's
86	/// the convention crucible-scoped tabtargets exploit when imprinting (e.g.,
87	/// `rbw-cC.Charge.{fixture}.sh`). Non-crucible fixtures (regime-*, calibrant-*,
88	/// canonical-*, etc.) carry their fixture name in this slot too, but no
89	/// nameplate-shaped consumer reads them.
90	pub struct rbtdri_Context {
91	    pub(crate) project_root: PathBuf,
92	    pub(crate) fixture: String,
93	    pub(crate) burv_temp_root: PathBuf,
94	    pub(crate) burv_output_root: PathBuf,
95	    pub(crate) invoke_count: u32,
96	    /// One-shot flag: when set, the NEXT invoke reuses the immediately-prior
97	    /// invoke's BURV root instead of minting a fresh one (see
98	    /// `chain_next_invoke`). Consumed and cleared by `rbtdri_invoke_impl`.
99	    pub(crate) chain_next: bool,
100	}
101	
102	impl rbtdri_Context {
103	    pub fn new(
104	        project_root: &Path,
105	        fixture: &str,
106	        burv_temp_root: &Path,
107	        burv_output_root: &Path,
108	    ) -> Self {
109	        Self {
110	            project_root: project_root.to_path_buf(),
111	            fixture: fixture.to_string(),
112	            burv_temp_root: burv_temp_root.to_path_buf(),
113	            burv_output_root: burv_output_root.to_path_buf(),
114	            invoke_count: 0,
115	            chain_next: false,
116	        }
117	    }
118	
119	    pub fn fixture(&self) -> &str {
120	        &self.fixture
121	    }
122	
123	    pub fn project_root(&self) -> &Path {
124	        &self.project_root
125	    }
126	
127	    /// Mark the NEXT tabtarget invocation to chain off the immediately-prior
128	    /// invoke's BURV root, rather than running in fresh isolation.
129	    ///
130	    /// Theurge gives every invoke its own `BURV_OUTPUT_ROOT_DIR`, so
131	    /// `bud_dispatch`'s start-of-dispatch `current/`->`previous/` promotion never
132	    /// crosses invokes — each invoke's `previous/` is empty. That suits isolated
133	    /// operations but breaks the depth-1 cross-tabtarget chain a real operator
134	    /// gets for free by sharing one `../output-buk` root: the chaining fact one
135	    /// tabtarget writes to `current/` never reaches the next tabtarget's
136	    /// `previous/`. The bole derived-pull base-anchor election is the consumer —
137	    /// `ensconce` writes the touchmark to `current/`, the following `ordain`
138	    /// reads it from `previous/`.
139	    ///
140	    /// Calling this before such a pair makes the next invoke reuse the prior
141	    /// invoke's root, so the prior invoke's `current/` is promoted into this
142	    /// invoke's `previous/` — replicating the operator flow for exactly the
143	    /// invokes that need it, leaving every other invoke's isolation intact.
144	    /// One-shot: consumed by the next invoke and cleared. Depth-1 only — bud
145	    /// keeps a single generation, so only the immediate predecessor is visible.
146	    pub fn chain_next_invoke(&mut self) {
147	        self.chain_next = true;
148	    }
149	
150	    /// Read the suite-monotonic BURV invoke counter. The suite loop reads it
151	    /// after each fixture and seeds the next Context, so per-invoke dir names
152	    /// stay unique across fixtures (see set_invoke_count).
153	    pub fn invoke_count(&self) -> u32 {
154	        self.invoke_count
155	    }
156	
157	    /// Seed the BURV invoke counter so this fixture's invokes continue the
158	    /// suite-monotonic sequence rather than restarting at 0. Crate-internal code
159	    /// mutates the field directly; the bin crate must go through this setter
160	    /// because the field is pub(crate) and so not visible across the lib/bin
161	    /// boundary.
162	    pub fn set_invoke_count(&mut self, count: u32) {
163	        self.invoke_count = count;
164	    }
165	}
166	
167	// ── Tabtarget discovery ──────────────────────────────────────
168	
169	/// Find the tabtarget script for a colophon + imprint (nameplate or role).
170	///
171	/// Scans tt/ for files matching `{colophon}.*.{imprint}.sh`.
172	/// Returns error if zero or multiple matches — exactly one must exist.
173	pub fn rbtdri_find_tabtarget(
174	    project_root: &Path,
175	    colophon: &str,
176	    imprint: &str,
177	) -> Result<PathBuf, String> {
178	    let tt_dir = project_root.join("tt");
179	    let prefix = format!("{}.", colophon);
180	    let suffix = format!(".{}.sh", imprint);
181	
182	    let entries = std::fs::read_dir(&tt_dir)
183	        .map_err(|e| format!("rbtdri: cannot read tt/ directory: {}", e))?;
184	
185	    let matches: Vec<PathBuf> = entries
186	        .filter_map(|entry| entry.ok())
187	        .map(|entry| entry.path())
188	        .filter(|path| {
189	            if let Some(name) = path.file_name().and_then(|n| n.to_str()) {
190	                name.starts_with(&prefix) && name.ends_with(&suffix)
191	            } else {
192	                false
193	            }
194	        })
195	        .collect();
196	
197	    match matches.len() {
198	        0 => Err(format!(
199	            "rbtdri: no tabtarget for colophon '{}' imprint '{}'",
200	            colophon, imprint
201	        )),
202	        1 => Ok(matches.into_iter().next().unwrap()),
203	        n => Err(format!(
204	            "rbtdri: {} tabtargets match colophon '{}' imprint '{}' — expected exactly one",
205	            n, colophon, imprint
206	        )),
207	    }
208	}
209	
210	/// Find a global tabtarget (no imprint suffix).
211	///
212	/// Scans tt/ for files matching `{colophon}.{frontispiece}.sh` (exactly two dots).
213	/// Rejects files with imprint suffixes (three+ dots).
214	pub fn rbtdri_find_tabtarget_global(
215	    project_root: &Path,
216	    colophon: &str,
217	) -> Result<PathBuf, String> {
218	    let tt_dir = project_root.join("tt");
219	    let prefix = format!("{}.", colophon);
220	
221	    let entries = std::fs::read_dir(&tt_dir)
222	        .map_err(|e| format!("rbtdri: cannot read tt/ directory: {}", e))?;
223	
224	    let matches: Vec<PathBuf> = entries
225	        .filter_map(|entry| entry.ok())
226	        .map(|entry| entry.path())
227	        .filter(|path| {
228	            if let Some(name) = path.file_name().and_then(|n| n.to_str()) {
229	                if name.starts_with(&prefix) && name.ends_with(".sh") {
230	                    // Global: no imprint — exactly one part between colophon and .sh
231	                    let middle = &name[prefix.len()..name.len() - 3]; // strip ".sh"
232	                    !middle.contains('.')
233	                } else {
234	                    false
235	                }
236	            } else {
237	                false
238	            }
239	        })
240	        .collect();
241	
242	    match matches.len() {
243	        0 => Err(format!(
244	            "rbtdri: no global tabtarget for colophon '{}'",
245	            colophon
246	        )),
247	        1 => Ok(matches.into_iter().next().unwrap()),
248	        n => Err(format!(
249	            "rbtdri: {} global tabtargets match colophon '{}' — expected exactly one",
250	            n, colophon
251	        )),
252	    }
253	}
254	
255	// ── Tabtarget invocation with BURV isolation ─────────────────
256	
257	static RBTDRI_BASH_PROGRAM: OnceLock<String> = OnceLock::new();
258	
259	/// The bash program theurge launches scripts with, resolved once per process.
260	///
261	/// On Cygwin a bare `"bash"` from a Windows-native binary resolves through
262	/// `CreateProcess` to System32's WSL launcher, never Cygwin's bash — so we
263	/// nativize the canonical Cygwin bash (`/bin/bash`) via RBTDRX's cygpath. That
264	/// keeps cygpath inside theurge's existing Rust dependency rather than kit bash,
265	/// and cygpath itself resolves correctly from a native binary (no System32 twin,
266	/// unlike bash). Off Cygwin — and as a fallback if cygpath fails — it is "bash".
267	pub fn rbtdri_bash_program() -> &'static str {
268	    RBTDRI_BASH_PROGRAM
269	        .get_or_init(|| {
270	            if rbtdrx_is_cygwin() {
271	                match rbtdrx_posix_to_native(RBTDRI_CYGWIN_BASH_POSIX) {
272	                    Ok(p) => p.to_string_lossy().into_owned(),
273	                    Err(_) => "bash".to_string(),
274	                }
275	            } else {
276	                "bash".to_string()
277	            }
278	        })
279	        .as_str()
280	}
281	
282	/// Build a `Command` that launches a tabtarget — a bash `.sh` — portably.
283	///
284	/// A Windows-native theurge (`x86_64-pc-windows-gnu`) cannot `CreateProcess` a
285	/// `.sh` directly: Rust's `Command` on Windows launches only `.exe`. So we run
286	/// the script through bash — via `rbtdri_bash_program()` so the right bash is
287	/// chosen on Cygwin — with the script path rendered to POSIX form (RBTDRX). On
288	/// Linux/macOS that conversion is identity and the bash program is just "bash",
289	/// so the call site is unconditional. Callers chain `.args(...)`,
290	/// `.current_dir(...)`, and `.env(...)` as on any `Command::new` result.
291	pub fn rbtdri_tabtarget_command(tabtarget: &Path) -> Command {
292	    let mut cmd = Command::new(rbtdri_bash_program());
293	    cmd.arg(rbtdrx_native_to_posix(tabtarget));
294	    cmd
295	}
296	
297	/// Internal: execute a resolved tabtarget with BURV isolation and optional extra env vars.
298	///
299	/// Honors the context's one-shot `chain_next` flag (see
300	/// `rbtdri_Context::chain_next_invoke`): when set, this invoke reuses the
301	/// immediately-prior invoke's BURV root instead of minting a fresh one — so
302	/// `bud_dispatch` promotes that invoke's `current/` into this invoke's
303	/// `previous/`. The flag is consumed and cleared here.
304	fn rbtdri_invoke_impl(
305	    ctx: &mut rbtdri_Context,
306	    tabtarget: &Path,
307	    args: &[&str],
308	    extra_env: &[(&str, &str)],
309	) -> Result<rbtdri_InvokeResult, String> {
310	    let invoke_num = if std::mem::take(&mut ctx.chain_next) {
311	        // Chain off the immediately-prior invoke: reuse its root (do NOT mint a
312	        // fresh one or bump the counter), so bud's promotion carries that
313	        // invoke's current/ into this one's previous/. Depth-1 by construction.
314	        ctx.invoke_count.checked_sub(1).ok_or_else(|| {
315	            "rbtdri: chain_next_invoke set with no prior invoke to chain from".to_string()
316	        })?
317	    } else {
318	        let n = ctx.invoke_count;
319	        ctx.invoke_count += 1;
320	        n
321	    };
322	
323	    let dir_name = rbtdri_invoke_dir_name(invoke_num);
324	    let burv_output = ctx.burv_output_root.join(&dir_name);
325	    let burv_temp = ctx.burv_temp_root.join(&dir_name);
326	
327	    std::fs::create_dir_all(&burv_output)
328	        .map_err(|e| format!("rbtdri: failed to create BURV output dir: {}", e))?;
329	    std::fs::create_dir_all(&burv_temp)
330	        .map_err(|e| format!("rbtdri: failed to create BURV temp dir: {}", e))?;
331	
332	    let mut cmd = rbtdri_tabtarget_command(tabtarget);
333	    cmd.args(args)
334	        .current_dir(&ctx.project_root)
335	        .env("BURV_OUTPUT_ROOT_DIR", rbtdrx_native_to_posix(&burv_output))
336	        .env("BURV_TEMP_ROOT_DIR", rbtdrx_native_to_posix(&burv_temp));
337	
338	    for (key, value) in extra_env {
339	        cmd.env(key, value);
340	    }
341	
342	    let output = cmd
343	        .output()
344	        .map_err(|e| format!("rbtdri: failed to execute '{}': {}", tabtarget.display(), e))?;
345	
346	    Ok(rbtdri_InvokeResult {
347	        stdout: String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stdout).into_owned(),
348	        stderr: String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stderr).into_owned(),
349	        exit_code: output.status.code().unwrap_or(-1),
350	        burv_output,
351	    })
352	}
353	
354	/// Invoke a fixture-imprinted tabtarget (colophon + ctx.fixture). For crucible
355	/// fixtures the fixture name is also a nameplate moniker, which is the
356	/// imprint shape this resolves against.
357	pub fn rbtdri_invoke(
358	    ctx: &mut rbtdri_Context,
359	    colophon: &str,
360	    args: &[&str],
361	) -> Result<rbtdri_InvokeResult, String> {
362	    let tabtarget = rbtdri_find_tabtarget(&ctx.project_root, colophon, &ctx.fixture)?;
363	    rbtdri_invoke_impl(ctx, &tabtarget, args, &[])
364	}
365	
366	/// Invoke a fixture-imprinted tabtarget (like `rbtdri_invoke`) with extra
367	/// environment variables threaded into the child process — e.g. BURD_NO_LOG
368	/// to keep BUK dispatch from folding the tabtarget's stderr into stdout.
369	pub fn rbtdri_invoke_env(
370	    ctx: &mut rbtdri_Context,
371	    colophon: &str,
372	    args: &[&str],
373	    extra_env: &[(&str, &str)],
374	) -> Result<rbtdri_InvokeResult, String> {
375	    let tabtarget = rbtdri_find_tabtarget(&ctx.project_root, colophon, &ctx.fixture)?;
376	    rbtdri_invoke_impl(ctx, &tabtarget, args, extra_env)
377	}
378	
379	/// Invoke a global tabtarget (no imprint) with optional extra environment variables.
380	pub fn rbtdri_invoke_global(
381	    ctx: &mut rbtdri_Context,
382	    colophon: &str,
383	    args: &[&str],
384	    extra_env: &[(&str, &str)],
385	) -> Result<rbtdri_InvokeResult, String> {
386	    let tabtarget = rbtdri_find_tabtarget_global(&ctx.project_root, colophon)?;
387	    rbtdri_invoke_impl(ctx, &tabtarget, args, extra_env)
388	}
389	
390	/// Invoke a tabtarget with an explicit imprint (overrides ctx.fixture for discovery).
391	pub fn rbtdri_invoke_imprint(
392	    ctx: &mut rbtdri_Context,
393	    colophon: &str,
394	    imprint: &str,
395	    args: &[&str],
396	) -> Result<rbtdri_InvokeResult, String> {
397	    let tabtarget = rbtdri_find_tabtarget(&ctx.project_root, colophon, imprint)?;
398	    rbtdri_invoke_impl(ctx, &tabtarget, args, &[])
399	}
400	
401	// ── BURV fact file reading ───────────────────────────────────
402	
403	/// Read a fact file from a tabtarget's BURV output directory.
404	/// Fact files are single-line values written by tabtargets to BURD_OUTPUT_DIR,
405	/// which is BURV_OUTPUT_ROOT_DIR/current per BUK dispatch convention.
406	pub fn rbtdri_read_burv_fact(
407	    result: &rbtdri_InvokeResult,
408	    fact_name: &str,
409	) -> Result<String, String> {
410	    let path = result.burv_output.join(RBTDRI_BURV_OUTPUT_SUBDIR).join(fact_name);
411	    let content = std::fs::read_to_string(&path)
412	        .map_err(|e| format!("rbtdri: cannot read fact '{}' from {}: {}", fact_name, path.display(), e))?;
413	    let trimmed = content.trim().to_string();
414	    if trimmed.is_empty() {
415	        return Err(format!("rbtdri: fact '{}' is empty in {}", fact_name, path.display()));
416	    }
417	    Ok(trimmed)
418	}
419	
420	/// Enumerate multi-fact files in a tabtarget's BURV output directory.
421	/// Multi-facts follow the convention `<root>.<ext>` written by buf_write_fact_multi;
422	/// returns the sorted list of roots whose files have the requested extension.
423	/// Returns an empty Vec if no matching files exist.
424	pub fn rbtdri_read_burv_facts_multi(
425	    result: &rbtdri_InvokeResult,
426	    extension: &str,
427	) -> Result<Vec<String>, String> {
428	    let dir = result.burv_output.join(RBTDRI_BURV_OUTPUT_SUBDIR);
429	    let entries = std::fs::read_dir(&dir).map_err(|e| {
430	        format!(
431	            "rbtdri: cannot enumerate fact dir {}: {}",
432	            dir.display(),
433	            e
434	        )
435	    })?;
436	    let suffix = format!(".{}", extension);
437	    let mut roots: Vec<String> = entries
438	        .filter_map(|entry| entry.ok())
439	        .filter_map(|entry| entry.file_name().into_string().ok())
440	        .filter_map(|name| name.strip_suffix(&suffix).map(str::to_string))
441	        .collect();
442	    roots.sort();
443	    Ok(roots)
444	}
445	
446	// ── Ifrit verdict parsing ────────────────────────────────────
447	
448	/// Ifrit verdict wire protocol: ifrit prints exactly one line matching
449	/// `IFRIT_VERDICT: PASS` or `IFRIT_VERDICT: FAIL <detail>` to stdout.
450	/// Missing verdict line is always a failure — no silent pass-through.
451	pub fn rbtdri_parse_ifrit_verdict(stdout: &str, exit_code: i32) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
452	    for line in stdout.lines() {
453	        let trimmed = line.trim();
454	        if let Some(rest) = trimmed.strip_prefix("IFRIT_VERDICT:") {
455	            let rest = rest.trim();
456	            if rest.starts_with("PASS") {
457	                return rbtdre_Verdict::Pass;
458	            }
459	            if let Some(detail) = rest.strip_prefix("FAIL") {
460	                let detail = detail.trim();
461	                if detail.is_empty() {
462	                    return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail("ifrit reported failure".to_string());
463	                }
464	                return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(detail.to_string());
465	            }
466	        }
467	    }
468	
469	    if exit_code == 0 {
470	        rbtdre_Verdict::Fail("ifrit exited 0 but no verdict line found".to_string())
471	    } else {
472	        rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("ifrit exited {} with no verdict line", exit_code))
473	    }
474	}
475	
476	/// Invoke the ifrit binary inside a charged bottle via the bark tabtarget.
477	/// The attack selector argument tells ifrit which attack module to run.
478	/// Returns a test verdict based on ifrit's stdout verdict line and exit code.
479	pub fn rbtdri_invoke_ifrit(
480	    ctx: &mut rbtdri_Context,
481	    attack_selector: &str,
482	) -> Result<rbtdre_Verdict, String> {
483	    let result = rbtdri_invoke(ctx, RBTDGC_CRUCIBLE_BARK, &[RBTDRI_IFRIT_BINARY, attack_selector])?;
484	    Ok(rbtdri_parse_ifrit_verdict(&result.stdout, result.exit_code))
485	}
486

1	#!/bin/bash
2	#

12	# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
13	# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
14	# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
15	# limitations under the License.
16	#
17	# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
18	#
19	# Recipe Bottle Auth - RBRA/RBRO credential load and role token mint
20	
21	set -euo pipefail
22	
23	# Multiple inclusion detection
24	test -z "${ZRBA_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rba multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
25	ZRBA_SOURCED=1
26	
27	######################################################################
28	# Internal Functions (zrba_*)
29	
30	zrba_kindle() {
31	  test -z "${ZRBA_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rba already kindled"
32	
33	  # Ensure dependency kindled first (rba mints tokens via rbgo)
34	  zrbgo_sentinel
35	
36	  readonly ZRBA_KINDLED=1
37	}
38	
39	zrba_sentinel() {
40	  test "${ZRBA_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rba not kindled - call zrba_kindle first"
41	}
42	
43	######################################################################
44	# External / RBTOE Pattern Functions
45	
46	rba_get_governor_token_capture() {
47	  zrba_sentinel
48	
49	  # Need access to RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE from regime
50	  test -n "${RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE:-}" || return 1
51	
52	  local z_token
53	  z_token=$(rbgo_get_token_capture "${RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE}") || return 1
54	
55	  test -n "${z_token}" || return 1
56	  echo    "${z_token}"
57	}
58	
59	rba_authenticate_role_capture() {
60	  zrba_sentinel
61	
62	  local -r z_rbra_file="${1}"
63	
64	  test -n "${z_rbra_file}" || return 1
65	  test -f "${z_rbra_file}" || return 1
66	
67	  buc_log_args "Authenticating with RBRA file: ${z_rbra_file}"
68	
69	  source "${z_rbra_file}" || return 1
70	
71	  local z_token
72	  z_token=$(rbgo_get_token_capture "${z_rbra_file}") || return 1
73	
74	  test -n "${z_token}" || return 1
75	
76	  echo "${z_token}"
77	}
78	
79	rba_extract_json_to_rbra() {
80	  zrba_sentinel
81	
82	  local -r z_json_path="$1"
83	  local -r z_rbra_path="$2"
84	  local -r z_lifetime_sec="$3"
85	  local -r z_expected_project_id="${4:-}"
86	
87	  test -f "${z_json_path}" || buc_die "Service account JSON not found: ${z_json_path}"
88	
89	  buc_info "Extracting service account credentials from JSON"
90	
91	  buc_log_args 'Extract fields'
92	  local z_client_email
93	  z_client_email=$(jq -r '.client_email' "${z_json_path}") \
94	                                        || buc_die "Failed to extract client_email"
95	  test -n "${z_client_email}"           || buc_die "Empty client_email in JSON"
96	  test    "${z_client_email}" != "null" || buc_die "Null client_email in JSON"
97	
98	  local z_private_key
99	  z_private_key=$(jq -r '.private_key' "${z_json_path}") \
100	                                       || buc_die "Failed to extract private_key"
101	  test -n "${z_private_key}"           || buc_die "Empty private_key in JSON"
102	  test    "${z_private_key}" != "null" || buc_die "Null private_key in JSON"
103	
104	  local z_project_id
105	  z_project_id=$(jq -r '.project_id' "${z_json_path}") \
106	                                      || buc_die "Failed to extract project_id"
107	  test -n "${z_project_id}"           || buc_die "Empty project_id in JSON"
108	  test    "${z_project_id}" != "null" || buc_die "Null project_id in JSON"
109	
110	  if test -n "${z_expected_project_id}"; then
111	    buc_log_args "Verify project matches expected: ${z_expected_project_id}"
112	    test "${z_project_id}" = "${z_expected_project_id}" \
113	      || buc_die "Project mismatch: JSON has '${z_project_id}', expected '${z_expected_project_id}'"
114	  else
115	    buc_log_args "No project validation - accepting JSON project_id: ${z_project_id}"
116	  fi
117	
118	  buc_log_args 'Write RBRA file'
119	  # CAUTION: jq -r unescapes JSON \n to real newlines, so z_private_key holds a
120	  # multi-line PEM string. printf '%s' below preserves real newlines into the
121	  # RBRA file. Consumer (rbgo_oauth.sh:zrbgo_build_jwt_capture) tolerates either
122	  # real-newline or '\n'-escape form via printf '%b'; do not "normalize" to one
123	  # form without auditing the consumer.
124	  {
125	    printf 'RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL="%s"\n'      "${z_client_email}"
126	    printf 'RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY="'; printf '%s' "${z_private_key}"; printf '"\n'
127	    printf 'RBRA_PROJECT_ID="%s"\n'        "${z_project_id}"
128	    printf 'RBRA_TOKEN_LIFETIME_SEC=%s\n'  "${z_lifetime_sec}"
129	  } > "${z_rbra_path}" || buc_die "Failed to write RBRA file ${z_rbra_path}"
130	
131	  test -f "${z_rbra_path}" || buc_die "Failed to write RBRA file: ${z_rbra_path}"
132	
133	  buc_warn "Consider deleting source JSON after verification: ${z_json_path}"
134	}
135	
136	# RBTOE: RBRA Load Pattern
137	# Sources an RBRA file and validates required fields
138	rba_rbra_load() {
139	  zrba_sentinel
140	
141	  local -r z_rbra_file="${1}"
142	
143	  test -n "${z_rbra_file}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA file path required"
144	  test -f "${z_rbra_file}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA file not found: ${z_rbra_file}"
145	
146	  buc_log_args "Loading and validating RBRA credentials from ${z_rbra_file}"
147	
148	  # Source the RBRA file
149	  source "${z_rbra_file}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: failed to source RBRA file"
150	
151	  # Validate required fields
152	  test -n "${RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL:-}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL missing from ${z_rbra_file}"
153	  test -n "${RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY:-}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY missing from ${z_rbra_file}"
154	  test -n "${RBRA_PROJECT_ID:-}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_PROJECT_ID missing from ${z_rbra_file}"
155	
156	  # Check for null values
157	  test "${RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL}" != "null" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL is null in ${z_rbra_file}"
158	  test "${RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY}" != "null" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY is null in ${z_rbra_file}"
159	  test "${RBRA_PROJECT_ID}" != "null" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_PROJECT_ID is null in ${z_rbra_file}"
160	
161	  buc_log_args "RBRA validation successful: ${RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL} in project ${RBRA_PROJECT_ID}"
162	}
163	
164	# RBTOE: RBRO Load Pattern
165	# Thin wrapper: defensively sources rbro_regime.sh (callers don't need to know
166	# its path, which moved under AAD's payor/ subdirectory migration), then
167	# delegates to rbro_load. Parallels rba_rbra_load at the call-signature level
168	# even though that function carries its own validation; the uniform rba_*
169	# load-through-utility convention is the load-bearing reason this wrapper exists.
170	rba_rbro_load() {
171	  zrba_sentinel
172	
173	  buc_log_args "Loading RBRO OAuth credentials"
174	
175	  # Source regime module if not already loaded
176	  if test -z "${ZRBRO_SOURCED:-}"; then
177	    source "${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}/rbro_regime.sh"
178	  fi
179	
180	  # Delegate to regime's canonical load
181	  rbro_load
182	
183	  buc_log_args "RBRO validation successful"
184	}
185	
186	# eof

#!/bin/bash
#
# Copyright 2025 Scale Invariant, Inc.
#
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#
# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
#
# Recipe Bottle Auth - RBRA/RBRO credential load and role token mint

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion detection
test -z "${ZRBA_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rba multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
ZRBA_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (zrba_*)

zrba_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBA_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rba already kindled"

  # Ensure dependency kindled first (rba mints tokens via rbgo)
  zrbgo_sentinel

  readonly ZRBA_KINDLED=1
}

zrba_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBA_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rba not kindled - call zrba_kindle first"
}

######################################################################
# External / RBTOE Pattern Functions

rba_get_governor_token_capture() {
  zrba_sentinel

  # Need access to RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE from regime
  test -n "${RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE:-}" || return 1

  local z_token
  z_token=$(rbgo_get_token_capture "${RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE}") || return 1

  test -n "${z_token}" || return 1
  echo    "${z_token}"
}

rba_authenticate_role_capture() {
  zrba_sentinel

  local -r z_rbra_file="${1}"

  test -n "${z_rbra_file}" || return 1
  test -f "${z_rbra_file}" || return 1

  buc_log_args "Authenticating with RBRA file: ${z_rbra_file}"

  source "${z_rbra_file}" || return 1

  local z_token
  z_token=$(rbgo_get_token_capture "${z_rbra_file}") || return 1

  test -n "${z_token}" || return 1

  echo "${z_token}"
}

rba_extract_json_to_rbra() {
  zrba_sentinel

  local -r z_json_path="$1"
  local -r z_rbra_path="$2"
  local -r z_lifetime_sec="$3"
  local -r z_expected_project_id="${4:-}"

  test -f "${z_json_path}" || buc_die "Service account JSON not found: ${z_json_path}"

  buc_info "Extracting service account credentials from JSON"

  buc_log_args 'Extract fields'
  local z_client_email
  z_client_email=$(jq -r '.client_email' "${z_json_path}") \
                                        || buc_die "Failed to extract client_email"
  test -n "${z_client_email}"           || buc_die "Empty client_email in JSON"
  test    "${z_client_email}" != "null" || buc_die "Null client_email in JSON"

  local z_private_key
  z_private_key=$(jq -r '.private_key' "${z_json_path}") \
                                       || buc_die "Failed to extract private_key"
  test -n "${z_private_key}"           || buc_die "Empty private_key in JSON"
  test    "${z_private_key}" != "null" || buc_die "Null private_key in JSON"

  local z_project_id
  z_project_id=$(jq -r '.project_id' "${z_json_path}") \
                                      || buc_die "Failed to extract project_id"
  test -n "${z_project_id}"           || buc_die "Empty project_id in JSON"
  test    "${z_project_id}" != "null" || buc_die "Null project_id in JSON"

  if test -n "${z_expected_project_id}"; then
    buc_log_args "Verify project matches expected: ${z_expected_project_id}"
    test "${z_project_id}" = "${z_expected_project_id}" \
      || buc_die "Project mismatch: JSON has '${z_project_id}', expected '${z_expected_project_id}'"
  else
    buc_log_args "No project validation - accepting JSON project_id: ${z_project_id}"
  fi

  buc_log_args 'Write RBRA file'
  # CAUTION: jq -r unescapes JSON \n to real newlines, so z_private_key holds a
  # multi-line PEM string. printf '%s' below preserves real newlines into the
  # RBRA file. Consumer (rbgo_oauth.sh:zrbgo_build_jwt_capture) tolerates either
  # real-newline or '\n'-escape form via printf '%b'; do not "normalize" to one
  # form without auditing the consumer.
  {
    printf 'RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL="%s"\n'      "${z_client_email}"
    printf 'RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY="'; printf '%s' "${z_private_key}"; printf '"\n'
    printf 'RBRA_PROJECT_ID="%s"\n'        "${z_project_id}"
    printf 'RBRA_TOKEN_LIFETIME_SEC=%s\n'  "${z_lifetime_sec}"
  } > "${z_rbra_path}" || buc_die "Failed to write RBRA file ${z_rbra_path}"

  test -f "${z_rbra_path}" || buc_die "Failed to write RBRA file: ${z_rbra_path}"

  buc_warn "Consider deleting source JSON after verification: ${z_json_path}"
}

# RBTOE: RBRA Load Pattern
# Sources an RBRA file and validates required fields
rba_rbra_load() {
  zrba_sentinel

  local -r z_rbra_file="${1}"

  test -n "${z_rbra_file}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA file path required"
  test -f "${z_rbra_file}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA file not found: ${z_rbra_file}"

  buc_log_args "Loading and validating RBRA credentials from ${z_rbra_file}"

  # Source the RBRA file
  source "${z_rbra_file}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: failed to source RBRA file"

  # Validate required fields
  test -n "${RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL:-}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL missing from ${z_rbra_file}"
  test -n "${RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY:-}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY missing from ${z_rbra_file}"
  test -n "${RBRA_PROJECT_ID:-}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_PROJECT_ID missing from ${z_rbra_file}"

  # Check for null values
  test "${RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL}" != "null" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL is null in ${z_rbra_file}"
  test "${RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY}" != "null" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY is null in ${z_rbra_file}"
  test "${RBRA_PROJECT_ID}" != "null" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_PROJECT_ID is null in ${z_rbra_file}"

  buc_log_args "RBRA validation successful: ${RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL} in project ${RBRA_PROJECT_ID}"
}

# RBTOE: RBRO Load Pattern
# Thin wrapper: defensively sources rbro_regime.sh (callers don't need to know
# its path, which moved under AAD's payor/ subdirectory migration), then
# delegates to rbro_load. Parallels rba_rbra_load at the call-signature level
# even though that function carries its own validation; the uniform rba_*
# load-through-utility convention is the load-bearing reason this wrapper exists.
rba_rbro_load() {
  zrba_sentinel

  buc_log_args "Loading RBRO OAuth credentials"

  # Source regime module if not already loaded
  if test -z "${ZRBRO_SOURCED:-}"; then
    source "${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}/rbro_regime.sh"
  fi

  # Delegate to regime's canonical load
  rbro_load

  buc_log_args "RBRO validation successful"
}

# eof
